label: no
cot_prefix: Okay, so the user is asking if a man can legally leave all his money to his widow's sister, whom he plans to marry next year. Let me start by breaking this down. First, the man is currently married. He has a wife, and he's leaving all his money to her sister, whom he's planning to marry after his current wife passes. That seems a bit odd, but maybe there's a specific legal arrangement here. I need to think about inheritance laws. In many jurisdictions, the distribution of a deceased person's estate is governed by either the deceased's will or, if there's no will, by intestacy laws. So, if the man has a will that leaves everything to his future wife (his current wife's sister), then the question is whether that will is valid. But here's the catch: the man is currently married to someone else. If he writes a will that leaves everything to his future wife, is that permissible? Legally, can someone make a will that benefits someone they are not yet married to? In many places, you can name anyone in your will, regardless of your relationship, as long as you have testamentary capacity and the will is properly executed. So if the man is of sound mind and the will is properly drawn up and signed, then yes, he can name his future wife as a beneficiary. However, there might be complications if he and the sister are not yet married at the time of his death. For instance, if the will states he is leaving everything to his "future wife" or to a specific person who he intends to marry, but they are not married by the time he dies, then the designation might not be valid, or the estate might be distributed to other heirs by default. Another angle is the possibility of a prenuptial agreement or postnuptial agreement. If the sister is someone he's going to marry, maybe there's an agreement in place that would address inheritance rights. However, that's more about the sister's legal rights in the future marriage rather than the current will.
